overview.info:
Target: 10.129.44.186
Scope: this is a ctf
this is a ctf

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.

blocks.name: Host Posture
blocks.goal: Collect host configuration and Linux security posture clues.

blocks.name: Service Inventory
blocks.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.
blocks.selection_rationale: Kept nmap_scan and route_topology as they are allowed and useful for service/route mapping. Replaced dns_recon (not in allowed tools) with a scoped dig command to gather basic DNS info without brute-forcing.
blocks.summary: Service inventory block completed for target 10.129.44.186 (CTF linux_server). Two open TCP services identified (SSH on port 22, HTTP on port 80) and a DNS hostname '2million.htb' observed. Route topology probing failed to establish connectivity or latency metrics, while DNS query returned no records (NXDOMAIN).
key_findings: Host 10.129.44.186 is up, resolves to hostname '2million.htb'.
No DNS records found for the IP (NXDOMAIN).
Route topology tools failed to gather path or latency data.
risk_signals: Exposed SSH (port 22) and HTTP (port 80) services may present attack surface.
Hostname '2million.htb' suggests potential virtual hosting; web service may respond to this name.
open_questions: Are SSH credentials or keys available for authentication?
Does the HTTP service on port 80 serve content for '2million.htb'?
Are there additional services on non-standard ports not covered by top-100 scan?
artifacts: Hostname: 2million.htb
Open ports: 22/tcp (SSH), 80/tcp (HTTP)

results.summary: Nmap TCP scan confirmed host 10.129.44.186 is up with two open ports: 22 (SSH) and 80 (HTTP). Hostname '2million.htb' observed.
artifacts: 2million.htb
22/tcp (SSH)
80/tcp (HTTP)

results.summary: Route topology tools (MTR, Nmap traceroute) failed to gather path or latency data; host connectivity could not be mapped beyond local network.
DNS dig query for ANY records on 10.129.44.186 returned NXDOMAIN; no records found.

blocks.name: Host Posture
blocks.goal: Collect host configuration and Linux security posture clues.
blocks.selection_rationale: linux_config_audit is allowed and directly relevant for assessing Linux host posture. No refinement needed.
blocks.summary: The host posture assessment for 10.129.44.186 failed due to a configuration error in the linux_config_audit tool. The tool requires a username for remote target auditing, which was not provided.
key_findings: The host posture assessment for 10.129.44.186 failed due to a configuration error in the linux_config_audit tool. The tool requires a username for remote target auditing, which was not provided.
open_questions: What is the correct username for remote auditing of this Linux host?
Are there alternative tools or methods to assess host posture without requiring a username?

results.summary: Tool execution failed due to missing username for remote target auditing. No host posture data was collected.

program.name: Service Inventory
program.goal: Map open services, routes, and transport exposure.

tools.reason: Basic DNS record check for the target IP to complement service mapping.

program.selection_rationale: Kept nmap_scan and route_topology as they are allowed and useful for service/route mapping. Replaced dns_recon (not in allowed tools) with a scoped dig command to gather basic DNS info without brute-forcing.

program.name: Host Posture
program.goal: Collect host configuration and Linux security posture clues.
program.selection_rationale: linux_config_audit is allowed and directly relevant for assessing Linux host posture. No refinement needed.

updates.summary: No evidence of authentication weaknesses, JWT flaws, or session mismanagement was found in the login page, JavaScript file, or prior tool outputs. The original finding lacks reproducible security impact.

updates.title: Test for IDOR or broken access control on user-specific endpoints of '2million.htb'
updates.summary: All tested user-specific endpoints returned a 301 redirect to a generic 404 page without exposing unauthorized user data or unauthorized access, confirming no IDOR or broken access control vulnerability.

updates.title: Assess impact of missing or weak security headers on '2million.htb'
updates.summary: The target is missing critical security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, etc.), enabling risks like XSS, clickjacking, and MIME-sniffing. Verified via direct header analysis.

items.name: Add '2million.htb' to /etc/hosts and verify HTTP service response for this hostname
Perform full TCP port scan (1-65535) to identify non-standard open ports
Perform UDP port scan (top 1000) to identify exposed services
Enumerate SSH version and supported authentication methods on port 22
Check for HTTP service version, headers, and technologies (e.g., Server, X-Powered-By, Cookies)
Review HTTP response for '2million.htb' for clues (e.g., comments, hidden paths, API endpoints)
Test for virtual host misconfiguration (e.g., default server response, IP-based access)
Check for exposed .git, .svn, or other VCS directories on the web root
Spider/crawl the web application to map all accessible routes and parameters
Test for common web vulnerabilities (e.g., SQLi, XSS, LFI, RFI) on identified endpoints
Review authentication mechanisms (e.g., login forms, session cookies, JWT tokens)
Check for exposed sensitive files (e.g., backup files, configuration files, database dumps)
Test for HTTP verb tampering and method-based vulnerabilities (e.g., PUT, DELETE)
Assess impact of missing or weak security headers (e.g., CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
Test for IDOR or broken access control on user-specific endpoints
Test for weak or default credentials on SSH service (e.g., root:toor, admin:admin)
Check for SSH key-based authentication support and enumerate potential key paths
Test for SSH version-specific vulnerabilities (e.g., user enumeration, CVE exploits)
Validate if SSH brute-forcing is feasible (e.g., rate-limiting, account lockout)
If initial access is gained, enumerate user privileges and sudo capabilities
Check for writable system files or directories (e.g., /etc/passwd, cron jobs)
Search for sensitive files (e.g., SSH keys, configuration files, credentials)
Review running processes and services for privilege escalation opportunities
Search for flags in common locations (e.g., /home, /var/www, /root)
Document all findings, exploit paths, and proof of concepts for reporting

verified_findings.title: Hardcoded API endpoint (/api/stats/daily/owns/) and hidden parameters were confirmed in the JavaScript file, validating the finding as a real vulnerability.
verified_findings.summary: Deepen enumeration of web application endpoints to identify hidden parameters or routes using manual and automated techniques
